coredata.dc:title: Boosted photocatalytic activity of LaFeO3/Ag3PO4 heterojunction via carbon quantum dots: Higher conductivity, stability, and dispersivity

coredata.dc:description: Several restrictions including low photon conversion efficiency, unabiding material stability, and aggregation behavior hinder large scale applicability of conventional semiconductor photocatalysts for pollutants removal. In this work, the inexpensive carbon quantum dots (CQDs) was synthesized by facile one-step hydrothermal process and introduced into LaFeO3/Ag3PO4 nanocomposites for photodegradation of organic dyes. The morphology, surface chemical states, optical and electrochemical properties of as-prepared samples were systematically investigated using HRTEM, FT-IR, XPS, UV–vis,UV–vis, and PL. LaFeO3/Ag3PO4 with 3 wt% of CQDs presented the higher photocatalytic activity with 97.3 % removal rate of methylene blue (MB) organic dyes under 60 min visible light irradiation, which were three times that of the binary LaFeO3/Ag3PO4 composites. The eﬀect of initial pH and co-existing inorganic ions on MB removal eﬃciency, particle aggregation behavior during photocatalytic process, and dominant reaction active species were investigated. The results showed that CQDs significantly improved the photoelectrochemical (PEC) performance of LaFeO3/Ag3PO4 composites, as well as protected Ag3PO4 from photo corrosion due to variation of electronic transfer path in composite material. Benefitting from numerous oxygen and nitrogen-containing functional groups, CQDs as a dispersing agent, improved water dispersibility and wettability of photocatalyst particles, and inhibited agglomeration of nano-photocatalyst on photocatalytic reactivity. These findings highlight the potential utility of CQDs in assisting a rapidly photodegrading molecular dye in an aqueous solution.

head.ce:title: Boosted photocatalytic activity of LaFeO3/Ag3PO4 heterojunction via carbon quantum dots: Higher conductivity, stability, and dispersivity

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Several restrictions including low photon conversion efficiency, unabiding material stability, and aggregation behavior hinder large scale applicability of conventional semiconductor photocatalysts for pollutants removal. In this work, the inexpensive carbon quantum dots (CQDs) was synthesized by facile one-step hydrothermal process and introduced into LaFeO3/Ag3PO4 nanocomposites for photodegradation of organic dyes. The morphology, surface chemical states, optical and electrochemical properties of as-prepared samples were systematically investigated using HRTEM, FT-IR, XPS, UV–vis,UV–vis, and PL. LaFeO3/Ag3PO4 with 3 wt% of CQDs presented the higher photocatalytic activity with 97.3 % removal rate of methylene blue (MB) organic dyes under 60 min visible light irradiation, which were three times that of the binary LaFeO3/Ag3PO4 composites. The eﬀect of initial pH and co-existing inorganic ions on MB removal eﬃciency, particle aggregation behavior during photocatalytic process, and dominant reaction active species were investigated. The results showed that CQDs significantly improved the photoelectrochemical (PEC) performance of LaFeO3/Ag3PO4 composites, as well as protected Ag3PO4 from photo corrosion due to variation of electronic transfer path in composite material. Benefitting from numerous oxygen and nitrogen-containing functional groups, CQDs as a dispersing agent, improved water dispersibility and wettability of photocatalyst particles, and inhibited agglomeration of nano-photocatalyst on photocatalytic reactivity. These findings highlight the potential utility of CQDs in assisting a rapidly photodegrading molecular dye in an aqueous solution.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Photocatalytic degradation via semiconductor-based nano-photocatalysis is regarded as one of the most efﬁcient and environment-friendly strategies to address escalating dye-containing wastewater treatment. Nevertheless, limitations of low irradiation efficiency due to the opacity of the organic dyes slurry and the decomposition of the catalyst particles are an inescapable impediment to practical feasibility [1–3]. Furthermore, inorganic nano-photocatalysis exhibits a strong tendency to agglomerate during the photocatalytic process due to surface charge and van der Waals forces [4]. According to research, the formation of aggregates can cause blue shifts in the absorption spectrum as well as a decrease in specific surface area, which can lead to weakness of electro-optical property and photocatalytic reactivity of photocatalysts [5,6]. As a result, the coupling of eﬃcient light-harvesting and high structural stability with high water dispersibility of nano-photocatalysis is especially critical for applications in heterogeneous photocatalytic processes.

Perovskite oxide (ABO3-type) based materials have emerged as promising photocatalyst materials owing to their outstanding structural stability, superior light-harvesting properties, and good cost-eﬀect [7–10]. Unlike most perovskite oxide materials, LaFeO3 is capable of harvesting the visible light due to its narrow band gap (~2.1Ev), which endows it with photocatalytic performance in the longer wavelength region of the solar spectrum [11–13]. On the other hand, a low band gap causes more pronounced recombination of photogenerated charges and insufficient positive and negative reduction potential to decompose organic pollutants [14,15]. It is an effective strategy to construct heterojunction interfaces by incorporating other semiconductor materials with appropriate band positions. Ag3PO4 is the most researched material in this class because of its approximately 90 % quantum efficiency from 400 to 480 nm, which is higher than the other reported semiconductors [16,17]. With a direct bandgap of 2.43 eV, Ag3PO4 is capable of harvesting the visible light with higher separation efficiency of carriers due to a built-in electric ﬁeld between silver and phosphate ions [18,19]. For photodegradation towards pollutants, studies of Ag3PO4 coupling with other semiconductors, such as Cr-SrTiO3, NaTaO3, and SrTiO3 have been intensively conducted [20–22]. Nevertheless, the desired removal efficiency could only be achieved if the composite system contained a high mass proportion of Ag3PO4, which would inevitably accelerate the photocorrosion rate of Ag3PO4 and reduce the light absorption efﬁciency. To that end, despite significant effort devoted to the incorporation of perovskite oxides with Ag3PO4 as catalysts, there are still cost-effectiveness and durability limits for practical applications.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a relatively low-cost carbon nanomaterial made up of sp2-hybridized graphitic carbon surrounded by amorphous carbon frames [23]. CQDs have been extensively utilized as light-harvesting materials and electron-transport channels for photocatalytic applications owing to their superior optical absorption and optoelectronic properties [24–29]. Because of superior performance in trapping and transferring electrons, CQDs can increase the electronic conductivity of heterostructure catalysts and effectively inhibit the recombination of photo-generated charges. It was also discovered that the appropriate bandgap of conjugated π-domains of CQDs assisted in suppressing the intrinsic defect and electron leakage from the perovskite material [30,31]. Meanwhile, numerous studies have shown that the surface chemical groups of CQDs (eg. hydroxyl, carboxyl, epoxy, amino, etc.) contributed to an increase in the wettability of photocatalysis. Kim et al. discovered that CQDs could aid in extinguishing the soaking effect on the perovskite material and increasing device stabilities due to their adequate down-conversion properties [32]. As a result, it is expected that the introduction of CQDs will effectively overcome defects of nano-photocatalysis for the photodegradation of organic dyes.

For this purpose, CQDs were prepared through a bottom-up strategy and implanted on the surface of LaFeO3/Ag3PO4 heterostructure nano-composites to photodegrade Methylene blue (MB) under visible-light irradiation. As expected, after the introduction of CQDs, LaFeO3/Ag3PO4 nano-composites exhibited superior PEC performance and higher the stability and dispersibility in the photocatalytic process. CQDs reduce the mass ratio of silver phosphate in catalytic materials without sacrificing the photocatalytic activity of binary heterojunction. According to spectroscopic characterizations, the relationship between photocatalytic activities, energy band alignments, and charge transfer was thoroughly investigated. Furthermore, a possible photocatalytic degradation mechanism of LaFeO3/Ag3PO4/ CQDs for decomposition of MB was proposed.

CQDs were prepared using a bottom-up strategy with citrate acid and CO(NH2)2 as carbon sources. In a typical process, 1.2 g CO(NH2)2 and 0.65 g citrate were dissolved in 40 mL ultrapure water and transferred into a Teﬂon-lined autoclave for hydrothermal treatment under air at 160 °C for 4 h. After cooling, a brown-yellow solution was obtained, which was centrifuged for 30 min at 10,000 rpm. The CQDs solution was further purified in the dark for about 24 h using 1000 Da dialysis tubing. The obtained orange-yellow solution was freeze-dried to a powder for future use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of porous LaFeO3, LaFeO3/Ag3PO4 and LaFeO3/Ag3PO4/CQDs photocatalysts
ce:section.ce:para: A modified hydrothermal technique was used to make porous LaFeO3. Briefly, stoichiometric concentrations of La(NO3)30.6 H2O and Fe(NO3)30.9 H2O (molar ratio=1:1) were dissolved in 80 mL of deionized water. Subsequently, The solution was then supplemented with 3.6 g of glycine and 1.2 g of PVP (MW 10,000). After 20 min of magnetic stirring, 160 mL of NH4OH was added to correct the pH to 9.1, and the solution was sealed in a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. The material was rinsed with DI water and centrifuged for 10 min at 900 rpm. After that, the porous LaFeO3 particles were created by drying them for 2 h in a vacuum oven at 65 °C.

In the silver-ammine solution, photocatalyst LaFeO3/Ag3PO4 with various mass ratios was produced using an in-situ precipitation approach. A certain amount of as-prepared LaFeO3 was dispersed in 30 mL of deionized water by ultrasonic treatment for 20 min. Then, under continuous magnetic stirring for 10 min, 0.3654 g AgNO3 solid particles were dissolved into the aforementioned mixture. Finally, a 20 mL NaH2PO4 solution was mixed with the aforesaid mixture and stirred for 10 h. To obtain a LaFeO3/Ag3PO4 sample, the obtained precipitates were washed with DI water and ethanol and dried in air at 60 °C overnight. According to the amounts of LaFeO3 in the composite system, the series of photocatalysts prepared were labeled 20 %-LA, 50 %-LA, and 80 %-LA, respectively. The hydrothermal technique was used to introduce CQDs on the surface of 80 %-LA to create the hybrid photocatalyst LaFeO3/Ag3PO4/CQDs. Typically, 0.99 g of 80 %-LA powder was dispersed into 60 mL ethanol, then specified volumes of CQDs were introduced dropwise into the ethanol and sonicated for another 2 h. The mixture was evaporated and dried for 4 h at 100 ℃. Finally, a series of LaFeO3/Ag3PO4/CQDs catalysts with varied mass contents of CQDs (1, 3, and 5 wt%) were prepared and designated as LAC1, LAC3, and LAC5 composite after being calcined at 260 ℃ for 2 h.

High-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) images were acquired by JEOL 2100 Cryo-electron microscope at a 300 kV accelerating voltage. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were acquired by an Ultimate IV diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.15406 nm) in the 2θ-range from 20° to 80° with a step of 0.02°. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) studies were characterized on an ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer with an Mg-Ka radiation. The UV–vis diffused reflectance spectra (UV–vis DRS) were characterized on PE lambda 750 s UV-Vis spectrophotometer with a wavelength range of 200–800 nm The electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) signal acquired with a Bruker A300 electron paramagnetic resonance spectrometer was recorded to capture radicals. Photoluminescence spectra (PL) were measured using an Agilent Technologies Cary luminescence spectrometer. The specific surface area and pore size distribution of samples were recorded on a Quantachrome Monosorb physisorption apparatus (NOVA2000 Gas Sorption Analyzer). The transient photocurrent and Electrochemical impedance tests were performed on an electrochemical workstation (CHI660E) in a typical three-electrode configuration with the prepared photocatalyst working electrode, Pt wire counter electrode, and Ag/AgCl reference electrode, respectively. 0.1 mol/L Na2SO4 aqueous solution was used as the electrolyte.

The photocatalytic activities of as-prepared materials were evaluated sufﬁciently by degradation of MB under irradiation from a 300 W Xenon lamp (CHF-XM300, Trusttech) equipped with an AM 1.5 G filter (simulated AM 1.5 G illumination of 100 mW/cm2). Prior to light irradiation, a photocatalyst (50 mg) solution in MB (100 mL, 10 mg/L) was continuously stirred for 30 min to achieve adsorption equilibrium. After exposing simulated solar light in a photo-reactor, a 2 mL sample of the reaction suspension was withdrawn at predetermined time intervals and centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 3 min to separate the catalysts. High-performance liquid chromatography with a photodiode array detector was used to determine the MB concentration (detection limit of 0.1 mg/L).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Particle size distribution and zeta potential measurement
ce:section.ce:para:
The particle size distribution and hydrodynamic diameter (Z-average) of the photocatalyst during catalytic reaction were measured using a light scattering unit on a Zetasizer 3000 HAS in dynamic light scattering mode (DLS). During the measurement, a total volume of 1 mL of photocatalyst-containing aqueous mixtures was withdrawn and then quickly inserted into the sample pool at the interval of 0, 10, 30 min after reaction, and the DLS measurements were performed immediately at a scattering angle of 90°. Using a ZetaPALS analyzer, the zeta potentials were calculated from the average electrophoretic mobilities (EPM) of photocatalyst (Brookhaven, NY). Specifically, 10 mg/L photocatalyst suspensions were placed in polystyrene vials before being filled with deionized water and MB solution. Then, the EPM was detected in the electric field at room temperature. The Gouy-Chapman-Stern double-layer rigid sphere model was used to convert EPM to zeta potential, which can be calculated by the Eq. (1):

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphologies and structural properties of photocatalysts
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of the as-prepared LAC3 hybrid photocatalyst was characterized by TEM, as shown in Fig. 1a and b. LAC3 samples have a uniform particle-like appearance with an average size of ~70 nm (Fig. S1). More information about the components and microstructure of LAC3 were identified by the HRTEM image (Fig. 1c). An obvious phase interface is formed with LaFeO3, Ag3PO4, and CQDs, confirming the successful synthesis of the LAC ternary composites. Two interplanar distances of 0.272 nm and 0.293 nm correspond to the (121) and (210) planes of LaFeO3 and Ag3PO4, respectively [33,34], which was consistent with the XRD patterns of standard LaFeO3 and Ag3PO4, respectively. Besides, the HRTEM image of CQDs (Fig. 1d) shows that the CQDs are scattered on the surface of LaFeO3/Ag3PO4 composites with good dispersion. CQDs have a size distribution ranging from 1 to 6 nm, with an average size of 3.36 nm. The results of EDS elemental mapping images (Fig. 1e–k) further confirm the successful embedding of CQDs into LaFeO3/Ag3PO4 composites because elements of La, Fe, Ag, O, and C are uniformly distributed on the surface of LAC3.

The N2 adsorption/desorption curves and pore size distributions for 80 %-LA and LAC3 were shown in Fig. S2. It can be seen that both adsorption-desorption curves of 80 %-LA and LAC3 belong to the type I and H3 hysteresis loop, indicating the presence of a capillary condensation mechanism in the adsorption process. Both materials are mesoporous materials with relatively narrow pore size distribution [35]. The BET speciﬁc surface areas of 80 %-LA and LAC3 are calculated to be 9.89 m2/g and 12.21 m2/g, respectively, suggesting that when LA composites are decorated with CQDs, there are no substantial increases in specific surface area. Compared to 80 %-LA composite, there was a small increase in the breadth of the hysteresis loop of LAC3, indicating that the desorption process on the LAC3 surface became slower. This was attributed to functionalization of surface owing to the numerous functional groups introduced by CQDs. The average pore diameters of 80 %-LA and LAC3 determined by Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) adsorption was 16.24 nm and 15.34 nm, respectively. Between 6 and 40 nm, the pore volume fraction increased from 48 % of 80 %-LA to 72 % of LAC3. The diffusion and transport of reactants was thought to be aided by a more uniform mesoporous size, which had a positive impact on reaction kinetics. As can be shown, surface modification of CQDs can significantly increase mesoporous pore size distribution and reactive sites for catalytic material specific binding.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) was used to analyze the crystal structures and phase composition of as-prepared materials, as illustrated in Fig. S3. All characteristic diffraction planes of LaFeO3 and Ag3PO4 can be observed in the synthesized 80 %-LA and LAC3, verifying the successful synthesis heterojunctions. The XRD spectrum of CQDs (Fig. S4) shows that they are amorphous with a feature centered at 27.2°, which can be attributed to the (002) reflection (d002 =0.34 nm) of graphitic-like large lattice spacing due to low crystallinity [36,37]. The FTIR spectra of as-synthesis components are also shown in Fig. S3. The O-H stretching vibrations of adsorbed water molecules in all materials are allocated to the band around 3400 cm−1. The emergence of a prominent band at 559 cm−1 in the 80 %-LA and LAC3 sample is attributed to the characteristic of Fe-O stretching vibrations. The usual vibrational bands associated with the PO4 3− group, such as symmetric P-O stretching vibrations, can be found at 989 cm−1, The asymmetric stretching vibrations of the P-O group can be found at 1075 cm−1 and 1407 cm−1, respectively. Peaks at 2909 cm−1 and 1335 cm−1 in the CQDs sample correspond to stretching and bending vibrations of the C-H group, respectively. The bands at 819 cm−1 and 1715 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of C-O bonds and CO bonds. In addition, N-H stretching vibration at 1550 cm−1 has also been recorded. These findings revealed that CQDs were rich in oxygen-containing functional groups (-COOH and -OH groups), indicating that they have exceptional aqueous solubility. In comparison to pure CQDs, the CO vibration peak of the LAC3 ternary composites moved from 1715 cm−1 to 1696 cm−1 after combining with LA. The change of the CO stretching vibration peak could be attributed to electron transport from perovskite metal ions to CQDs, lowering the carbonyl bond energy.

The influence of CQDs on the chemical composition and surface state of the LaFeO3/Ag3PO4 complex photocatalysts was investigated using XPS. In the LAC3 composite, the survey scan XPS spectra (Fig. S5) contain La3d, Fe2p, Ag3p, O1s, C1s, N1s, and P2p spin orbits. The XPS spectra of Fe 2p before and after CQDs loading on catalysts are shown in Fig. 2a. The main spectra of Fe 2p3/2 can be ﬁtted by two peaks situated at binding energies of 713.0 and 710.8 eV, corresponding to the Fe3+and Fe2+, respectively. The relative amount of surface Fe3+ rose from 62.1 % to 71.7 % after the addition of CQDs, implying electrons transfer from LaFeO3 to CQDs. Two peaks at 376.3–376.7 eV and 367.4–367.6 eV correspond to Ag 3d3/2 and Ag 3d5/2, respectively, as seen in Ag 3d spectra (Fig. 2b). Positive shifts are seen after combining Ag+ with CQDs, which could be attributed to the interaction between Ag+ and the functional groups of CQDs with high electronegativity. Three oxygen chemical states can be seen in the O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 2c). The lattice oxygen (Olatt), which includes highly oxidative oxygen species, has a first peak at roughly 529.8–530.4 eV. Surface oxygen is responsible for the second peak, which is around 531.2–531.8 eV. (Osurface, hydroxyl, or carbonate). While molecular water adsorbed on the surface is responsible for the binding energy of 532.3–532.8 eV. The Olatt exists in the crystal structure of lanthanum ferrite and silver phosphate. The Olatt peaks shift to higher binding energy in the presence of CQDs, which could be attributed to a drop in the electron density of LaFeO3 and Ag3PO4 caused by the surface binding interaction between composite catalysts and CQDs. Notably, after adding CQDs, the relative content of Osurface grew from 29.2 % to 33.5 %. The Osurface is thought to have contributed to the increased catalytic activity by releasing reactive oxygen species. In C 1 s spectrum (Fig. 2d), three peaks at 284.8, 286.2, and 288.5 eV are assigned to the C-C, C-O, and CO groups, respectively [38].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical and photoelectrochemical properties of photocatalysts
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, the optical properties of the CQDs were investigated by UV-Vis absorption and PL spectra ( Fig. 3a). The UV-Vis absorption spectra demonstrate strong optical absorption in the UV region with a typical absorption band located at 230 and 310 nm, which is assigned to the π-π * transition of the aromatic sp2 hybridized carbon atoms in the core and n-π * transition of CO bonds, respectively [39,40]. PL excitation spectrum exhibits the CQDs have the maximum excitation wavelength at 360 nm similar to a previous report [41]. The PL emission proﬁle shows emission intensity varies from 454 to 610 nm on shifting the excitation wavelength from 360 to 460 nm, which exhibits typical excitation-wavelength dependent behavior. It is generally believed that the PL emission originates from charge recombination on surface-conﬁned states of CQDs, which was induced by the hybridization structure of edge groups resulting from carboxyl and amide. The excitation-wavelength-dependent behavior could be induced by various size CQDs particles and excited electrons relax to different energy levels [42].

To explore the effects of CQDs on charge transfer kinetics, EIS Nyquist plots of LaFeO3, Ag3PO4, 80 %-LA, and LAC3 photocatalysts were recorded at 1.23 V vs. RHE under illumination (300 mW/cm2). As depicted in Fig. 3b. the radius of the arc curve decreased in the following order: LaFeO3 > 80 %-LA >Ag3PO4 >LAC3. In contrast to 80 %-LA, the Rct value of LAC3 decreased from 1298 to 423 Ω, suggesting that the surface decoration of CQDs signiﬁcantly enhanced charge separation. The transient photocurrent (I-t) curves of the above samples were also studied at potential 1.23 V vs. RHE under simulated chopped sunlight (Fig. 3c). The photocurrent density of LAC3 (2.0 μA/cm2) exhibited a substantial increase, which was about 3 times higher than that of 80 %-LA (0.7 μA/cm2). This further proved that CQDs can enhance the inhibition of recombination of photo-generated charges and prolong the lifetime of carriers. It’s worth noting that the abrupt transient spikes for the pristine LaFeO3 and Ag3PO4 at the beginning of the light-on are attenuated, indicating that the formation of heterojunction can greatly suppress the recombination of charge carriers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of MB degradation using different photocatalysts
ce:section.ce:para:
The photocatalytic oxidation performance of the LAC composites was evaluated by measuring the photodegradation of MB dye under visible light irradiation. Fig. 4a and Table. S1 shows the adsorption and removal eﬃciency of MB by diﬀerent photocatalysts under similar experimental conditions. It can be found that no significant MB adsorptions removal was observed by the pure phase catalyst and binary materials for the first 30 min. While the adsorption removal rate increase rapidly with the rise of the mass ratio of CQDs, where there was about 5.13 %, 9.02 % and 17.9 % of adsorption removal in the presence of CQDs, suggesting the introduction of CQDs can effectively improved catalytic interfacial reaction conditions. Because of high quantum efficiency and great photosensitivity of Ag3PO4, the photocatalytic degradation rate of MB increased with the mass ratio of Ag3PO4 in binary LA materials. Specifically, MB removal eﬃciency increased from 34.6 % to 87.2 % when the mass ratio of Ag3PO4 in binary LA materials increased from 20 % to 80 %, and the correspondent rate constant (k
app ) increased from 0.0128 to 0.0572 min−1, respectively. Nevertheless, incorporating CQDs can significantly improve its catalytic activity for MB degradation at a low Ag3PO4 mass ratio in composite materials. It could be found that MB degrading efficiency increased from 34.6 % to 55.5 %, 97.3 %, and 73.1 % after introducing 1 %, 3 %, and 5 % wt% of CQDs into 80 %-LA materials, respectively. The kinetic constant K is calculated using linear plots of ln (C0/C) vs irradiation time (t) (Fig. 4b), and the LAC3 composite exhibit the maximal reaction rate constant of 0.0886 min−1, which is about 6.92 times higher than the 80 %-LA composite (0.0128 min−1). The performance comparison with other photocatalyst boosted with CQDs reported in the literature for the photocatalytic decomposition of organic dyes was listed in Table. S2. These findings indicate that the introduction of CQDs into the LA photocatalyst system significantly improves their photocatalytic activity for organic pollutant degradation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of photocatalyst dosage and initial MB concentration
ce:section.ce:para: The inﬂuence of LAC3 dosage on MB removal was displayed in Fig. 4c. The degradation eﬃciency of MB was accelerated with the rise of LAC3 dosage from 0.1 to 0.5 g/L within 60 min when the dosage exceeds 1.0 g/L, there was no discernible additional enhancement for MB degradation. The likely reason is that the addition of a high-dose catalyst will increase the turbidity of the reaction solution, causing a lot of light scattering and affecting light absorption of photocatalyst. Therefore, 0.5 g/L was chosen as the ideal LAC3 dosage for whole experiment. The inﬂuence of initial MB concentrationﬁnal were monitored as observed in Fig. 4d. When the initial MB concentration was increased from 10 to 50 mg/L, the removal efficiency dropped from 97.3 % to 51.5 %. This is likely due to the fact that the degradation intermediates compete for limited adsorption and catalytic activity sites, and this competition will intensify as pollutant concentrations rise.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Eﬀect of initial pH and co-existing inorganic ions
ce:section.ce:para:
The eﬀect of initial pH (pH0) on MB removal eﬃciency in both 20 %-LA and LAC3 photocatalyst system were explored, as recorded in Table 1. In presence of 20 %-LA, the decomposition of MB dye within 30 min increased from 50.7 % to 89.8 % when pH0 ranged from 3 to 11, which indicated that acid solution presented signiﬁcant inhibition for MB degradation. In contrast, the LAC3 photocatalyst system considerably reduced the inhibitory effect of pH on the efficiency of MB removal, which resulted in a catalytic efficiency reduction of roughly 12.7 %. Generally, active species and the surface charge and ionization state of catalyst are highly correlated with the solution pH. The relationship between surface charge density and pH value of the two photocatalyst systems in deionized water was investigated, as shown in Fig. S6. The isoelectric point (pHiep) of 20 %-LA was around 9.2 in deionized water, demonstrating that 20 %-LA nanoparticles was positive charged when pH< 9.2 and was negatively charged when pH> 9.2. Since MB is a cationic dye, electrostatic repulsion interactions between positive charged photocatalyst and MB molecules would inhibit the adsorption process consequently aﬀect the degradation rate of MB. LAC3 nanoparticles was negatively charged all along when pH0 ranged from 3.3 to 10.8, and was more negatively with the increase of pH0. It is conceivable that the adsorption of MB is favored on a negatively charged surface. In addition, H+ competed with O2 in solution for photogenerated electrons and had scavenging on·OH (Eq. (2)), resulting in the suppression of active species and the reduction of MB degradation rate.
(2)
·OH +
H
+
+
e
−
→
H
2
O

The co-existing inorganic matters in the real wastewaters is usually considered as a sensitive factor for photocatalytic performance, so the eﬀect of anions and cations on MB degradation was determined in this section. The ionic strength of all co-existing ions was controlled at 0.01 M for better comparisons. As showed in Table 1, the inhibition eﬀect of NO3 - and SO4 2- ions on the degradation of MB in both photocatalyst system was slight and could be negligible. The adverse eﬀect caused by Cl− ions was signiﬁcant in 20 %-LA catalysis system, which could be attributed the fact that the formation of AgCl surface coating on the photocatalyst in the presence of dissolved Ag+.

and chloride anions, resulting in destruction of crystal structure of Ag3PO4 and restricted adsorption between photocatalyst and visible light, as well as further less active species. The introduction of HCO3 − ions presented more signiﬁcant inhibition for MB degradation in both photocatalyst system. It was also reported that there were competitive capture for radicals species by HCO3 − ions and MB molecular (Eq. (3)).
(3)
·OH + HCO 3 -
→
H
2
O + ·
CO
3 −

As expected, the inhibitory impact of coexisting cations on the degradation of MB presented more signiﬁcant in the presence of divalent cations compared to monovalent cations, which is likely due to the efﬁciency of divalent cations in neutralizing the surface charge of photocatalyst nanoparticles.

It is a prevailing view that the reusability of photocatalysts results from structure stability, thus few studies with regard to particle aggregation effect on photocatalytic reactivity in aqueous solution are available. Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) measurements were used to determine the particle size distribution and variation of particle dispersion index (PDI) of these two nanocomposites in MB solution during the photocatalytic process. Fig. S7 show the changes in particle equivalent hydraulic diameter (Z-average) after a reaction time of 0, 10, 30, and 60 min of visible light photocatalytic degradation of MB by 20 %-LA and LAC3 composites. The Stokes-Einstein equation can be used to calculate the equivalent hydraulic diameter
(4)
D =
k
B
T
3 π η d
Where, D is the diffusion coefficient, kB is the Boltzmann constant, T is the absolute temperature; η is viscosity, and d is the equivalent hydraulic diameter. For two photocatalyst systems, the equivalent hydraulic diameter increases with the continuing catalytic processes due to particle aggregation in aqueous solutions under van der Waals and electrostatic force, forming the so-called secondary particle size [43]. The equivalent hydraulic diameter of 20 %-LA rises from 227 to 1440 nm after 60 min, which is significantly more than the increase in LAC3 from 263 to 446 nm. The polydispersity index (PDI) can be used to analyze the dispersion degree of particle size of nanoparticles. Generally, A PDI of less than 0.08 is considered “monodisperse”, meaning that all particles have the same degree of polymerization. As shown in Fig. S8, The PDI of LAC3 nanocomposites kept less than 0.09, whereas the PDI of 20 %-LA composites increased from 0.103 to 0.574, indicating a transformation of "polydisperse". The formation of nano-photocatalyst particles aggregation is strongly dependent on particleproperties, such as particle size, surface charge and the water chemistry. As revealed in Fig. S6, at pH value of MB solution (30 mg/L, pH=9.4), LAC3 particles are highly charged compared with 20 %-LA, the high repulsion forces between photocatalyst particles is efﬁcient in preventing their aggregation according to DLVO theory. It is worth noting here that coordination cations released from MB compete with protons in solution for the surface group of nanoparticles in the presence of MB, leading to a decrease in negative surface charge and pHiep of 20 %-LA shifts to higher pH. In any case, CQDs increase the negative charge of nanoparticles and improve photocatalyst dispersion stability.

The structural stability of binary LaFeO3/Ag3PO4 composites in the photocatalytic process is mainly affected by the photocorrosion of Ag3PO4, which can be mainly attributed to its weak solubility in water (0.02 g/L) and semiconductor band structure [44,45]. The inhibition of CQDs on photo corrosion of Ag3PO4 was investigated using the XRD pattern of the 80 %-LA and LAC3 composite at the end of the five cycles, as is shown in Fig. 5a. Diffraction peaks of metallic silver were identified in the XRD pattern of the employed 80 %-LA, showing that CQDs can prevent Ag3PO4 from photo corrosion and improve the stability of photocatalytic materials when compared to LAC3. A comparison of cyclic photocatalytic degradation MB between 20 %-LA and LAC3 was made to investigate the improvement of CQDs on the reusability of composite catalytic materials (Fig. 5b). The results showed that the photocatalytic degradation rate of 20 %-LA for MB was found to have dropped by 48.9 % after five cycles, while LAC3 retained excellent photocatalytic activity. In addition, there was no obvious change in morphology of the LAC3 photocatalyst after photocatalytic degradation reaction (Fig. S9). As a dispersion agent combined with a photo corrosion inhibitor, CQDs boost the reusability of nano-catalyst in an aqueous solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Absorption measurements of the hybrid system
ce:section.ce:para:
The efficiency of dye molecules to stimulate charge transfer in semiconductor surfaces was significantly correlated with aggregation state [46–48]. In an aqueous solution, self-association of MB molecules results in two coexisting forms of monomers and dimers MB molecules (Eq. (5)), which can be easily identified spectroscopically.
(5)
2 MB
( monomers )
⇆ (
MB )
2 ( dimers )

The ratio of absorbance spectra of dimers (λ = 665 nm) and monomers (λ~610 nm) (Ad/Am) can be used to determine the degree of dimerization [49,50]. Absorbance spectra for MB (10 mg/L) at different catalyst dosages were recorded to investigate the enhancement of dye molecule aggregation by the CQDs on the photocatalyst interface ( Fig. 6a and b). In comparison to 80 %-LA, the shape of the absorption spectra of MB solution with LAC3 changes with a considerable rise at 610 nm and a reduction at 664 nm as the photocatalyst concentration increases from 0 to 1.0 g/L. As a result, the Ad/Am is plotted as a function of vesicle concentration (Fig. 6c), it can be seen that there is a small variation in Ad/Am of 80 %-LA. In contrast, the Ad/Am of MB solution with LAC3 reaches a maximum at a concentration of 1.0 g/L and subsequently starts to decrease. This drop is attributed to an increase in the number of binding sites, which lowers the local dye concentration in the interfacial region. Thus, an increase in mesoporous pore size distribution and specific binding reactive sites for catalytic material due to the introduction of CQDs to the 80 %-LA interface favors noncovalent dye binding to the interface and provides a large number of voids for stable dye dimerization, promoting dye adsorption and degradation interface reaction.

To identify the active species in the process of photocatalytic degradation MB, benzoquinone (BQ), Sodium oxalate(Na2C2O4), and isopropanol (IPA) were added to the photocatalytic reaction as the quenchers of·O- 2, h+ and·OH, respectively. As revealed in Fig. 7a, the dominating radical in the photocatalytic reaction in the LA catalytic system was·O- 2. The inhibitory impact of IPA (52.8 %) on MB degradation rates after CQDs implantation was higher than that of BQ (34.1 %), indicating that·OH was the major radical rather than that in the solution. EPR spectra were applied to further confirm the production of·O- 2 and·OH radicals (Fig. 7b and c). Under visible light, the six DMPO-·O- 2 characteristic signal peaks can be readily observed for both samples. The 20 %-LA sample displayed the stronger·O- 2 signals at different irradiation periods. In the LAC3 heterogeneous photocatalytic system [51], DMPO-·OH shows four signal peaks with a 1:2:2:1 intensity ratio, implying that the·OH is created with the injection of CQDs, which is compatible with trapping test results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Band gap structures and the possible degradation mechanism
ce:section.ce:para:
To gain insight into the charge redistribution behavior of photogenerated carriers in ternary LAC3 composite, the energy band alignments were determined by M-S tests and UV−vis DRS. The M-S plots of the pristine LaFeO3 and Ag3PO4 display a negative and positive slope respectively, meaning that the prepared pristine LaFeO3 and Ag3PO4 exhibit conduction characteristics of the p-type and n-type semiconductor (Fig. 7d and e) [52]. Furthermore, the inverted “V” shape M-S curve of prepared 80 %-LA also illuminates the fact that the p-n junction was formated in the binary heterojunction, as shown in Fig. 7f. The ﬂat band potentials (VFB) can be estimated according to the following Eq. (6):
(6)
1
C
2
=
2
e ε
ε
0
N
D
V −
V
FB
−
K
B
T
e

Here, C is the space charge capacitance, e is the electron charge, ε and ε0 are the relative dielectric constant and the permittivity of vacuum; KB is the Boltzmann constant. ND is the charge carrier densities. Obtained by the M-S plots, the ﬂat band potentials (VFB) of the pristine LaFeO3 and Ag3PO4 are 1.95 eV and 0.23 eV vs.RHE. Assuming potential diﬀerence both between the conduction band (CB) and VFB for n-type semiconductor and between the valence band (VB) and VFB for p-type semiconductor are 0.1 eV [53,54], the CB potentials of pristine Ag3PO4 and the VB potentials of pristine LaFeO3 are 0.13 eV and 2.05 eV vs. RHE, respectively. Furthermore, the charge carrier densities(ND) of 80 %-LA and LAC3 can be calculated by the slopes of M-S plots, of which the relative dielectric constant(ε) can be estimated by the Eq. (7) according to the impedance spectroscopy:
(7)
ε =
2 kd
Sf
Z
′ ′
where f and Z′′ are frequency and imaginary parts of impedance spectroscopy, and S and d are the area and thickness of photocatalytic films. The ND value of 80 %-LA and LAC3 is calculated to be 1.68 × 1018 and 4.02 × 1018 cm−3, respectively. implying LAC3 demonstrates the greater potential for charge carrier transfer and separation. The EF of p-type LaFeO3 and n-type Ag3PO4 can be determined to be 1.70 eV and 0.52 eV by the extrapolation of the abscissa int in the M-S plot. As expected, the UV−vis DRS indicates that the decoration of CQDs demonstrates its highest capability for visible light-harvesting (Fig. 7g). As revealed in the (αhν)2 versus the photon energy (hν) plot(Fig. 7h), the corresponding bandgap for the samples of LaFeO3, Ag3PO4, 80 %-LA, and LAC3 was calculated to be 2.04, 2.38, 2.08 and 1.94 eV, respectively. According to the bandgap, the VB potentials of Ag3PO4 and the CB potentials of LaFeO3 are 2.51 eV and 0.01 eV vs. RHE. The Lowest Unoccupied Molecular Orbital (LUMO) of CQDs can be estimated by the reduction peak at − 0.35 V vs. RHE via Cyclic Voltammetry (CV) measurement, as shown in Fig. 7i.

Based on the above analysis, the possible mechanism for enhanced photocatalytic activity of binary hybrid photocatalyst due to the addition of CQDs is elucidated in Scheme 1. For an 80 %-LA hybrid photocatalyst system, driven by visible light excitation and built-in electric fields of p-n junctions between Ag3PO4 and LaFeO3, the photogenerated electrons transfer from LaFeO3 cross interface to Ag3PO4, while the photogenerated holes flow in the opposite direction. Because of the high electrode potential of Ag/Ag3PO4 (0.45 V vs. NHE), electrons are more easily captured by the Ag+ released from the crystal lattice of Ag3PO4, resulting in the deposition of silver atoms on the surface of the catalyst. Subsequently, the photogenerated electrons then migrate to Ag through the Schottky contact between Ag and Ag3PO4 interface. Meanwhile, the electrons migrated to higher-energy states due to the surface plasmon resonance (SPR) phenomenon. Some electrons may be able to pass over the Schottky barrier and inject semiconductors, while others may be captured by O2 and converted to·O- 2 radicals, allowing MB to be oxidized directly on the surface. The enhanced catalytic activity of Ag3PO4 /LaFeO3 composite material constituted of a large mass ratio of silver phosphate may be due to silver atom precipitation in combination with the SPR effect. It should be emphasized that photo corrosion of silver phosphate degrades the structure of the material, resulting in a steady decrease in photocatalytic activity.

Upon the addition of CQDs on the surface of LA, CQDs first act as the center of photogenerated electron-hole recombination collecting the photogenerated electrons from Ag3PO4 and photogenerated holes from LaFeO3 through the ohmic contact in the Z-scheme patterns. A positive shift in the binding energy of Ag 3d XPS spectra (Fig. 4b) and no obvious Ag diffraction peaks in the XRD pattern of used LAC3 (Fig. S6) verify the change in electronic transfer path, which protects Ag3PO4 from photo corrosion and enhances the stability of photocatalytic materials. Thus, more powerful oxidative VB-holes of Ag3PO4 and reductive CB-electrons of LaFeO3 are preserved for forwarding oxidation and reduction reactions. The CB-electrons of LaFeO3 would be captured by MB molecules to form reduced radicals MB·, triggering a chain reaction beneficial for self-degradation. Because it is much higher than the potential energy of·OH generation (E(OH-/·OH)= 1.99 eV), the VB-holes of Ag3PO4 could oxidize OH- to produce·OH. CQDs can also absorb near-visible and UV light and directly excite the electrons in the HOMO into the LUMO to form electron-excited molecules. Beneﬁting from robust electron-extracting capacity,·O- 2 would be formed by the incorporation of electrons with O2 adsorbed on the surfaces of CQDs. Some of the photoexcited electrons relaxing into the energy levels of the surface state could be excited to high-energy orbitals again under visible light irradiation, enhancing the formation of·O- 2.

In this work, inexpensive CQDs were synthesized and used to boost the photocatalytic activity of LaFeO3/Ag3PO4 heterojunction nanocomposites in aqueous systems. Results extensively demonstrate the potential application of CQDs in photocatalytic schemes. Benefit from surface defect trapping sites, CQDs first act as an electron mediator, boosting the separation and migration of photoinduced hole-electron pairs at the junction interfaces of LaFeO3/Ag3PO4 through the ohmic contact in Z-scheme patterns. Furthermore, CQDs can directly transfer photoexcited electrons to electron acceptors O2 in aqueous via absorbing UV and visible light. Aside from optimizing energy band alignments and expediting the migration of electrons in heterostructured composite, the introduction of CQDs indirectly improves the overall photocatalysis process by adjusting the surface property of the photocatalyst. Numerous oxygen and nitrogen-containing functional groups located at the surface of photocatalyst improve its water dispersibility and wettability, and weaken the inhibition effect of secondary particle size on photocatalytic reactivity. The modified interfaces caused by loading CQDs affect the aggregation states of dye molecules around photocatalytic materials, accelerating adsorption performance as well as electron and energy transfer processes between them.
